title: 7 Tahsas — Archangel Gabriel & The Assembly
narrative:
The seventh day of Tahsas is consecrated above all to the Archangel Gabriel, the messenger of God, whose name in the Hebrew means 'man of God' or 'the strength of God,' and who has been from the foundations of sacred history the herald of the divine purposes concerning the Incarnation. It was Gabriel who appeared to Daniel with the seventy weeks of years that would culminate in the coming of the Anointed One; it was Gabriel who stood at the right hand of the altar of incense and announced to Zechariah the birth of the Forerunner; and it was Gabriel who descended to the little town of Nazareth and stood before the Virgin Mary with the supreme annunciation: 'Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with thee.' The Ethiopian Church honors Gabriel with particular fervor, and his feast on the twenty-second of each month is among the most beloved of the monthly commemorations.

Saint Ambrose of Milan appears again in this day's assembly, his name linked to the Archangel by the shared characteristic of divine eloquence, for as Gabriel bore the message of God to the Virgin, so Ambrose bore the message of God's full divinity and humanity to an empire wavering between orthodoxy and Arianism. The hymnody that Ambrose composed for the night offices of Milan became the foundation of Western liturgical song; his Te Deum and his metrical hymns carried the proclamation of the Incarnation into the mouths of ordinary Christians who might never read a theological treatise but who sang their theology with full hearts in the darkness of the winter vigils.

Saint Isidore of Chios, a young soldier-martyr of the Decian persecution, completes this day's assembly. Isidore's name in the Greek means 'gift of Isis,' a name given him in his pagan childhood but transformed by his conversion into a paradox: the one destined in his name for service to the old gods became instead a witness to the Lord of all gods. Stationed on the island of Chios, Isidore refused to sacrifice to the imperial idols, was subjected to terrible torments, and was beheaded; his relics were translated to Rome and became a source of healing for the faithful.

The theological seal of this seventh day of Tahsas is the mystery of divine speech entering human silence. Gabriel is the Voice of God dispatched to prepare the way; Ambrose is the human voice trained by grace to carry that divine word into the hearts of the faithful; Isidore is the witness who, when commanded to offer his voice in praise of idols, preferred silence and death to the betrayal of the Word. Together they proclaim that the Incarnation is above all a word spoken into the darkness — the eternal Word of the Father uttered in time, in flesh, in the cry of a child in Bethlehem.
